patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 22-year-old man. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry all the time, and I keep gaining weight even when I try to control it.” Early in the visit, volunteer: constant hunger/thoughts about food, easy weight gain for years, tired most days, and that you’ve always been shorter than peers.

Only if asked, share: weak muscles and delayed milestones as a baby, slow to feel full/never satisfied, sometimes sneaking food or eating at night, learning difficulties/needed school support, distress with routine changes, skin picking when stressed, low sweating, and rarely getting fevers.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about sleep, thirst, pain, puberty/sexual health, hands/feet size, or bowels: loud snoring/gasping and unrefreshed sleep, low thirst/forgetting to drink, low pain awareness, small hands/feet, low sex drive/limited puberty changes, frequent constipation.

Be reluctant and ashamed about taking food from others/trash or lying; admit only if the clinician asks gently and normalizes it. Express misconceptions: you suspect thyroid, Cushing’s, or “binge eating disorder.” If met with empathy, you open up more. Voice worries about cost/insurance, job privacy, and whether genetic/specialist care is “worth it.”

Start with: “I’ve had bad diarrhea with stomach cramps for over a week.” Volunteer only: 6–10 loose stools/day, crampy low belly pain that comes/goes, tired/weak, poor appetite. If asked for details, add: mucus and a little blood sometimes, urgency/tenesmus (feel you still need to go), on-and-off nausea without vomiting, low-grade feverish at night, a few pounds weight loss, recent travel with lots of street food. correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency (familial chylomicronemia syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Familial chylomicronemia syndrome due to APOC2/APOA5/GPIHBP1/LMF1 deficiency
Primary (monogenic) severe hypertriglyceridemia with chylomicronemia
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting triglycerides typically >1000 mg/dL (>11.3 mmol/L), often >2000 mg/dL
Low/normal apolipoprotein B (helps distinguish from familial combined hyperlipidemia/polygenic HTG where apoB is often elevated)
Clinical history consistent with chylomicronemia (e.g., recurrent abdominal pain/pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis) and onset from childhood/young adulthood
Exclusion of common secondary causes of severe hypertriglyceridemia (uncontrolled diabetes, alcohol excess, hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, pregnancy, triglyceride-raising drugs)
Lipid profile pattern consistent with chylomicrons: very high TG with relatively low/normal LDL-C and total cholesterol not proportionately elevated

expected_lab_results.Triglycerides (fasting): Severely elevated, typically >1000 mg/dL (>11.3 mmol/L), often >2000 mg/dL; may appear lipemic
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Normal to moderately elevated; not as high as triglycerides would suggest
expected_lab_results.Non-HDL cholesterol: Mild to moderately elevated; often less striking than triglycerides
expected_lab_results.Apolipoprotein B: Low or low-normal (often <75 mg/dL), supporting chylomicronemia rather than apoB-driven disorders
expected_lab_results.Lipase: Normal if no pancreatitis; elevated if concurrent acute pancreatitis
expected_lab_results.Amylase: Normal if no pancreatitis; elevated if concurrent acute pancreatitis (may be less sensitive than lipase)

non_pharmacological: Very-low-fat diet: target <10–15% of calories from fat (often 20–30 g/day), avoid large fat boluses; dietitian referral
Avoid alcohol completely
Avoid simple/refined carbohydrates and sugar-sweetened beverages; emphasize complex carbs and fiber
Weight management and regular physical activity as tolerated
Avoid triglyceride-raising medications when possible (e.g., estrogens, retinoids, certain antipsychotics, protease inhibitors, high-dose thiazides, nonselective beta-blockers)
Pancreatitis prevention counseling: seek urgent care for severe epigastric pain, vomiting, fever; maintain hydration
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck fasting triglycerides and full lipid panel in 4–8 weeks after dietary changes/therapy initiation, then every 3–6 months; immediate evaluation if symptoms suggest pancreatitis

must_include: Condition is genetic and causes impaired breakdown of triglyceride-rich particles (chylomicrons), leading to very high fasting triglycerides
Main clinical risk is acute pancreatitis (potentially life-threatening) rather than typical LDL-driven atherosclerosis

Why differentials are less likely: Sandhoff disease is a neurodegenerative lysosomal storage disorder; hereditary angioedema causes episodic swelling—neither explains isolated severe hypertriglyceridemia
Cornerstone is strict very-low-fat diet and alcohol avoidance to reduce chylomicrons and pancreatitis risk

If asked about specifics, mention falls when turning/uneven ground, shaky hands with fine tasks, eyes ‘jump’ side-to-side, occasional coughing with thin liquids, fatigue with walking, and that your dad had late-life “balance problems.” Do not volunteer urinary urgency/incontinence, lightheadedness on standing, numb/tingly feet, messier/larger handwriting, or avoiding stairs/dark rooms unless directly asked. patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 38-year-old male warehouse forklift operator on night shift. You speak minimally and act evasive, worried about missing work, costs, and getting fired. Start with: “My gums bleed and I’m getting big bruises for no reason, and I feel wiped out.” If asked for more, volunteer: bleeding gums with brushing, unexplained large bruises, extreme fatigue/weakness, lightheaded on standing, and low-grade fevers on/off for a week.

Only if directly asked, mention: frequent nosebleeds that take long to stop, tiny red/purple dots on lower legs, shortness of breath on stairs, night sweats, mouth sores/sore mouth, and fullness under left ribs after small meals. If asked about urine/bleeding, say you’ve noticed blood when wiping after peeing and occasional dark/tea-colored urine.

Hide unless specifically prompted: new headaches, intermittent blurry vision, tender swollen calf pain, and daily ibuprofen use. Resist discussing blood in stool, bleeding after sex, and increased alcohol unless reassured nonjudgmentally and confidentiality/cost concerns are addressed. Misattribute symptoms to “another respiratory infection,” hot-tub fungus, or needing stronger antibiotics. If met with empathy, you relax slightly but stay brief.

Peripheral smear.value: Numerous abnormal promyelocytes with Auer rods; occasional 'faggot cells'

Allopurinol 300 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
confounders: Thiopurine (mercaptopurine)-associated myelosuppression can mimic leukemia-related cytopenias

Allopurinol coadministration can increase mercaptopurine exposure, worsening cytopenias and complicating attribution

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), suspected
acceptable_alternatives: Acute myeloid leukemia with coagulopathy (until APL confirmed)
Disseminated intravascular coagulation secondary to acute leukemia
required_evidence: Peripheral smear showing abnormal promyelocytes with heavy granulation and/or Auer rods (often bundles/faggot cells)
Cytopenias (anemia and thrombocytopenia) with variable WBC and low/variable ANC
Coagulopathy consistent with DIC risk (prolonged PT/INR and/or aPTT, clinical bleeding concern)
High clinical suspicion warrants immediate ATRA initiation prior to genetic confirmation (PML-RARA testing not listed but should be urgently ordered)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Variable (can be low, normal, or elevated); may be abnormal for acute leukemia
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Often low or functionally ineffective; neutropenia may be present
expected_lab_results.Peripheral smear: Circulating promyelocytes/blasts; hypergranular promyelocytes and Auer rods; possible faggot cells
expected_lab_results.PT/INR: Prolonged or trending prolonged consistent with DIC risk
expected_lab_results.aPTT: May be prolonged (can be normal early but abnormality supports DIC risk)

medications: Tretinoin (ATRA) 45 mg/m^2/day PO in 2 divided doses immediately
Arsenic trioxide 0.15 mg/kg IV daily (initiate per risk stratification/oncology protocol once feasible)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12h at first sign/suspicion of differentiation syndrome (or prophylaxis per institutional protocol in high-risk features)
non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology/oncology consultation and treat as APL emergency; do not delay ATRA for confirmatory testing
Order urgent confirmatory testing for PML-RARA (FISH/RT-PCR) and bone marrow evaluation (even if not in listed labs)

Aggressive supportive care for coagulopathy: frequent monitoring and blood product support (target platelets >30–50k and correct coagulopathy per APL/DIC protocols; use cryoprecipitate/FFP as indicated)
Avoid procedures that increase bleeding risk until coagulopathy corrected (minimize venipunctures/IM injections)

If febrile or clinically infected with neutropenia: initiate empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics per neutropenic fever guidelines (select non-penicillin beta-lactam alternatives if true immediate penicillin allergy)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate inpatient management with q6–12h labs/coagulation reassessment initially; daily oncology-directed reassessment; outpatient follow-up within 24–72h after discharge with oncology

must_include: APL is a medical emergency because it can cause life-threatening bleeding from a clotting disorder (DIC-like coagulopathy)
Diagnosis is suspected from blood counts and smear; confirmation requires specialized testing (PML-RARA), but treatment must start immediately
Differentials considered (e.g., infections/bronchiectasis-related issues) are less consistent with the hematologic and coagulation findings
Start ATRA immediately even before confirmation to reduce bleeding risk and improve outcomes
Likely addition of arsenic trioxide (and/or chemotherapy depending on risk) under oncology guidance
Need for transfusions and close monitoring of clotting tests and blood counts
Warning signs of differentiation syndrome (fever, weight gain/edema, breathing difficulty, low blood pressure) and need for urgent steroids/notification
Discuss bleeding precautions and when to seek urgent help (new bruising, nosebleeds, gum bleeding, black stools, severe headache)
Discuss infection risk with low/ineffective neutrophils and to report fever immediately
Review penicillin allergy and how it affects antibiotic choices if infection treatment is needed
Review current mercaptopurine use and the need to reconcile/hold or adjust under oncology supervision due to myelosuppression risk
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless instructed due to bleeding risk
Avoid contact sports and minimize trauma; use soft toothbrush/electric razor
Medication adherence and not stopping ATRA/therapy without oncology guidance
Contraception/teratogenicity counseling for ATRA (avoid pregnancy; discuss contraception as appropriate)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key presenting symptoms and red flags (bleeding, infection, dyspnea), medication history (incl. mercaptopurine), pregnancy risk/contraception, and immediate safety concerns.

Order urgent labs to support suspected APL and assess coagulopathy: CBC with differential, peripheral smear review, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer, CMP (incl. LFTs/Cr), type & screen/crossmatch; consider uric acid/LDH and pregnancy test if applicable.
Review results for pancytopenia/leukocytosis with blasts/promyelocytes, Auer rods, and DIC-like pattern (low fibrinogen, prolonged PT, high D-dimer) to justify emergent management.
State suspected acute promyelocytic leukemia as a medical emergency; note confirmation requires PML-RARA testing but treatment should not wait.
Perform required critical allergy check before any prescribing (especially antibiotics/supportive meds); document penicillin allergy details and severity.
Perform required critical interaction check prior to prescribing ATRA/arsenic/supportive meds; pay attention to QT-prolonging agents, hepatotoxic drugs, and concurrent mercaptopurine.

Perform required critical contraindication check prior to prescribing: pregnancy/teratogenicity with ATRA, baseline QTc/electrolytes for arsenic, hepatic dysfunction, and other patient-specific contraindications.

Initiate ATRA immediately for suspected APL (do not wait for confirmation) and arrange urgent hematology/oncology-led regimen (arsenic trioxide ± chemotherapy based on risk); initiate/arrange transfusion and coagulation support orders per protocol.

Explain diagnosis urgency, treatment plan, differentiation syndrome warning signs, bleeding/infection precautions, medication adherence, avoidance of NSAIDs/aspirin, and contraception/teratogenicity counseling for ATRA.
Ensure immediate admission/transfer to oncology-capable facility/ICU-level monitoring as needed; schedule urgent hematology follow-up and serial labs (CBC/coags/electrolytes).
Close encounter after confirming emergent disposition, patient understanding, and clear return precautions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX’s parent bringing your 2-year-old daughter for “nonstop watery diarrhea and not gaining weight.” You are cooperative and speak clearly, worried but not hostile.

At the start, volunteer: watery diarrhea many times daily since early infancy, poor weight gain despite frequent feeding, tired/fussy most days, and hair that is thin/breaks easily and “woolly.”

If asked, add: poor appetite/gets full fast, bloated/gassy belly, frequent spit-up/vomiting, recurrent ear/chest infections needing antibiotics, dry rashy skin, severe diaper rash, occasional puffiness around eyes/ankles.

Do not volunteer unless specifically probed: stools sometimes greasy, very smelly, hard to clean; easy bruising/bleeding gums; slower milestones; occasional yellow-looking eyes when sick; mouth sores.

Be reluctant to admit you sometimes water down/stretch formula due to cost—only disclose if asked about feeding prep/food insecurity; express fear of judgment.

Share misconceptions: suspect milk allergy; relative mentions Crohn’s/colitis; tried lactose/gluten-free without real fix.

If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and ask about insurance coverage, cheaper formula alternatives, and whether spacing follow-ups is safe to reduce costs.

medications: Total parenteral nutrition (TPN) via central line continuous
Ursodeoxycholic acid 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
Zinc sulfate 1 mg/kg/day elemental zinc PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO daily
Oral rehydration solution PRN for diarrhea
Loperamide 0.08 mg/kg PO TID PRN (used sparingly under specialist guidance)
comorbidities: Failure to thrive with chronic watery diarrhea
Cholestatic liver disease with intermittent transaminitis
Central venous catheter dependence for nutrition
confounders: Celiac disease mimic (chronic diarrhea and poor growth despite negative serology)
Cow's milk protein allergy vs malabsorption causing diarrhea
Recurrent infections/inflammation can elevate fecal calprotectin, mimicking inflammatory bowel disease
TPN-associated cholestasis complicating attribution of liver enzyme abnormalities to the syndrome
Central line infections can cause intermittent fevers and elevated inflammatory markers

differentials: neonatal inflammatory skin and bowel disease
inflammatory bowel disease, immunodeficiency, and encephalopathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome (syndromic infantile diarrhea due to TTC37 or SKIV2L variants)
acceptable_alternatives: Syndromic infantile diarrhea (THES)
Congenital diarrheal disorder with hair abnormalities and liver disease
Very early onset inflammatory bowel disease (VEO-IBD) (initial working diagnosis pending genetic confirmation)
Primary immunodeficiency with enteropathy (initial working diagnosis pending genetic confirmation)
required_evidence: Severe early-onset/intractable diarrhea beginning in infancy with failure to thrive
Characteristic hair abnormalities (e.g., woolly/fragile hair; trichorrhexis nodosa on microscopy if available)
Evidence of liver involvement (elevated transaminases/cholestasis, hepatomegaly, or chronic liver disease features)
Exclusion/low likelihood of infectious causes and common malabsorption etiologies
Supportive features: facial dysmorphism, immunodeficiency/recurrent infections, growth restriction
Confirmatory testing: pathogenic variants in TTC37 or SKIV2L (or equivalent diagnostic genetic confirmation)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low or low-normal (anemia of chronic disease and/or iron deficiency from malabsorption)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Low or normal (microcytic if iron deficiency; may be normal early)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to elevated if intercurrent infection/inflammation; may be low if immunodeficiency component
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis with inflammation/malnutrition possible)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Sodium: Low or low-normal (hyponatremia possible due to chronic diarrhea)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Chloride: Low or normal (may be low with volume depletion)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Bicarbon: Low (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis from diarrheal bicarbonate loss)

medications: Oral rehydration solution 10 mL/kg PO after each loose stool (adjust to losses and tolerance)
Potassium chloride 0.5-1 mEq/kg PO/NG every 6-12 hours (titrate to serum K and ECG/clinical status)
non_pharmacological: Urgent pediatric gastroenterology and clinical genetics referral for confirmatory testing (TTC37/SKIV2L) and comprehensive evaluation
Nutritional rehabilitation with dietitian: high-calorie feeds; consider elemental/semi-elemental formula; assess for need for enteral tube feeding
If unable to maintain hydration/growth: initiate parenteral nutrition per pediatric GI/nutrition team
Monitor and correct dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities; strict intake/output and daily weights during acute management

Evaluate for and manage associated immunodeficiency: immunology referral; infection surveillance; ensure appropriate immunizations (avoid live vaccines if significant immunodeficiency suspected/confirmed)
Assess liver disease severity (LFTs, bilirubin, coagulation profile, ultrasound) and manage with hepatology as indicated
Consider endoscopy/biopsy only if needed to exclude VEO-IBD/other enteropathies; interpret in context of THES
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 24-72 hours for electrolyte/clinical reassessment if symptomatic or abnormal labs; otherwise specialist follow-up within 1-2 weeks and ongoing multidisciplinary follow-up (GI/nutrition/genetics/immunology) every 2-4 weeks initially.

must_include: This is a rare inherited condition that can cause severe diarrhea in infancy along with hair and liver problems
Why the team is considering it versus VEO-IBD/infection (pattern of symptoms and associated features)
Need for genetic testing to confirm and guide long-term care
Potential associated issues (growth/nutrition problems, infections/immunity, liver disease) and why multidisciplinary care is needed
Immediate priorities: hydration and correcting electrolytes/acid-base problems from diarrhea
Nutrition is a core therapy; may require specialized formula, tube feeds, or parenteral nutrition
Plan for specialist evaluations (GI, genetics, immunology, hepatology) and what each will address
Clear return precautions: reduced urine output, lethargy, persistent vomiting, blood in stool, fever, worsening dehydration
Discuss prognosis variability and that outcomes depend on nutrition support, infection prevention, and liver involvement
Review that antibiotics like amoxicillin-clavulanate caused a rash and should be avoided unless specifically evaluated
Invite questions about genetic implications for family and offer genetic counseling
How to track stools, hydration (wet diapers/urine output), weights, and signs of dehydration at home
Safe feeding plan as advised (formula type, volume goals, when to seek help if intolerance)
Infection prevention strategies and when to seek urgent care

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for chronic infantile diarrhea and associated features (onset, stool character, dehydration episodes, growth failure, infections, liver symptoms, hair changes, family history/consanguinity), plus current meds and prior reactions.
Order baseline evaluation for dehydration, electrolyte/acid-base derangements, malnutrition, liver disease, and infection/inflammation to support differential and immediate management.

Interpret labs to identify urgent abnormalities (hypovolemia, electrolyte disturbances, metabolic acidosis, hypoalbuminemia, transaminitis/cholestasis) and refine differential (infectious, VEO-IBD, congenital diarrheal disorders including tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome).
State working diagnosis (suspected tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome) and key differentials; note need for confirmatory genetic testing and multidisciplinary evaluation.
Before any prescribing, verify allergies and prior adverse drug reactions (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate rash) to prevent repeat exposure.
Before prescribing, screen for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions with any planned medications (non-critical but expected).

Initiate appropriate immediate management (rehydration plan and electrolyte correction; nutrition support plan and referrals). Avoid contraindicated/unsafe medications; do not prescribe antibiotics unless clear indication.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis rationale and need for genetic testing; explain treatment priorities and multidisciplinary plan; address prognosis, prior antibiotic rash, genetic counseling; provide home monitoring, feeding guidance, infection prevention, and return precautions.
Arrange timely follow-up with GI, genetics, immunology, and hepatology; set timeframe and ensure contingency planning if symptoms worsen.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and documenting plan and safety-netting.

Critical safety check before prescribing any medication or supplement (e.g., vitamin D, NSAIDs, acetaminophen).
Critical check before prescribing: renal/hepatic disease, pregnancy considerations, NSAID GI/bleeding risk, acetaminophen liver disease/alcohol use, hypercalcemia for vitamin D.
Non-critical but expected check before prescribing: interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., NSAIDs with anticoagulants, vitamin D with thiazides/digoxin).

Critical check before ordering any additional tests/procedures (e.g., EMG, MRI with contrast, biopsy referral) to ensure no contraindications (contrast allergy/renal function for gadolinium; bleeding risk for biopsy).

Arrange neuromuscular specialist referral for genetic counseling/testing and review of prior biopsy records; set follow-up to review labs, symptom trajectory, and response to conditioning/med changes.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

general_concerns: Believes scleroderma is always rapidly fatal and that organ failure is inevitable within a few years.
Thinks cold hands and color changes are just 'poor circulation' and not part of the disease, so warming gloves are optional rather than important.
Assumes skin tightening can be reversed quickly with moisturizers or 'collagen supplements' and is frustrated when that doesn’t happen.
Worries that taking ramipril means her kidneys are already failing, rather than understanding it may be used to protect kidney function and control blood pressure.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female administrative assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My fingers keep turning white and numb in the cold, and my hands feel tight and swollen.” Early in the visit, volunteer: fingers turn white/blue with cold or stress then tingle/hurt on warming; morning puffiness and tight skin with harder finger bending; morning stiffness/aches; nighttime heartburn/sour taste. If asked, add: fingertip sores/cracks slow to heal; food sticking/need water; shortness of breath on stairs; shiny thicker hand skin; tighter face/smaller mouth opening; dry cough for months. Do not mention unless specifically probed: avoiding cold drinks/freezer aisle, weight loss from uncomfortable eating, dry non-sweaty hands, bloating/constipation. Resist/disclose only if clinician directly asks about sexual or emotional impact: painful sex due to dryness; embarrassment/avoiding social situations. Express misconceptions: think it’s poor circulation/anemia; worry heartburn is ulcer/gallbladder; note OTC decongestant seemed to worsen episodes. If clinician suggests specialists/tests, worry they’ll be very expensive and try to delay. If offered generics, say you fear they’re less effective/unsafe and prefer brand-name. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and become more open.

medications: Ramipril 5 mg PO once daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO once daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO once daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO once daily
comorbidities: Iron deficiency anemia (likely from GI telangiectasias)
confounders: Long-standing Raynaud phenomenon causing episodic finger discoloration and pain (can mimic peripheral vascular disease)
GERD with intermittent dysphagia from esophageal dysmotility (can mimic primary reflux or functional dyspepsia)
Hypertension with rising creatinine and mild proteinuria while on ACE inhibitor (can obscure early scleroderma renal involvement vs medication-related changes)
Possible early pulmonary arterial hypertension suggested by elevated NT-proBNP and exertional dyspnea (symptoms can be attributed to anemia or deconditioning)

Evidence of internal organ involvement risk requiring screening (renal crisis risk; pulmonary involvement risk)
No alternative explanation better accounts for the presentation (e.g., SLE, RA, dermatomyositis, mixed connective tissue disease)
